assumption_register.assumption: The moving average is computed as a simple (arithmetic) moving average of monthly closing prices, not an exponential or weighted variant — no EMA or adaptive MA is substituted.
A single monthly close crossing the SMA triggers the signal; no consecutive-close or multi-bar confirmation filter is applied on entry or exit.
The S&P 500 used is the total-return index (including reinvested dividends), not the price-return index — otherwise cash-period missed dividends are a hidden drag.
The SMA is computed from a trailing window of exactly 10 monthly observations, equally weighted, with the current month's close included in the window that defines the signal for that same month.
Trades execute at the closing price of the monthly bar that triggers the signal (immediate execution, no look-ahead bias from using same-bar close to generate the signal).
During cash periods, capital earns the 90-day Treasury bill rate using the constant-maturity series (e.g., FRED DGS3MO), not a rolling auction-cycle or 13-week bill with decaying maturity.
The T-bill rate is accrued on a simple (not compounded) basis between monthly rebalancing points, matching the monthly signal cadence.
The cash-equivalent instrument is a 90-day T-bill specifically, not a money-market fund, cash deposit, or a different short-term government maturity.
No transaction costs, slippage, commissions, or bid-ask spreads are applied to index entries, exits, or bill rollovers — the backtest is friction-free.
No tax drag is modelled — all returns are pre-tax, as in a tax-exempt or retirement account.
The S&P 500 index does not change composition, methodology, or survivorship-bias characteristics over the test period; historical index data is treated as static.
The T-bill yield can fall to zero or negative without a behavioural or operational floor; the strategy does not impose a zero-rate floor or seek alternative cash instruments when rates are negative.
